claim: Obsidian 1.4 (July 2023) introduced the Properties UI for typed YAML frontmatter, addressing inconsistencies in property keys and values.
quote: Obsidian v1.4 brings a new UI for properties that may just solve some of those, including the difficulty in keeping frontmatter properties and keys consistent.

claim: Obsidian 1.9.0 released May 21, 2025 introduced Bases as a core plugin and broke support for deprecated singular properties (tag, alias, cssclass), now requiring plural list forms.
quote: tags, aliases, and cssclasses (plural forms) with values as lists. Previously supported singular forms (tag, alias, cssclass) no longer function

claim: Obsidian 1.10.0 (October 2025) added card view, Maps view, list view, and Bases APIs for custom view layouts.
quote: Obsidian 1.10.0 updates Bases with powerful new ways to visualize data, including a new Maps plugin that lets you see your entries on a map.

claim: Obsidian supports six built-in property types (Text, List, Number, Checkbox, Date, Date & Time) and three default reserved properties (tags, aliases, cssclasses).
quote: Text, List, Number, Checkbox, Date, Date & Time ... tags: List-type grouping by topic; aliases: Alternative note names; cssclasses: CSS snippet styling

claim: Bases ignores inline Dataview-style fields (Rating:: 5); a mandatory `type` property in YAML acts as the primary schema identifier for filtering.
quote: Obsidian Bases do not read, parse, or query the main content of a note ... Data that needs to be sorted, filtered, or displayed in a Base must exist as a formal property in the YAML frontmatter.

claim: Bases filters consist of Property + Operator + Value, support and/or/not logic, and views are configured graphically without code.
quote: Views require filters with three components: Property - the field to evaluate; Operator - comparison logic (is, contains, greater than, etc.); Value - the comparison target

claim: Recommended 2026 pattern is to keep canonical data in YAML frontmatter, use Bases for clickable/editable boards, and Dataview for ad-hoc queries and rollups.
quote: Keep all data in the same frontmatter properties; build interactive boards with Bases and reports/analytics with Dataview.

claim: Obsidian Properties does not support nested YAML, forcing flat schemas (e.g., series_name and series_num as siblings rather than under a series: parent).
quote: Obsidian Properties don't support nested YAML data

claim: Dataview has not received significant updates in a long time while Bases ships improvements regularly, making Bases the preferred path for new schemas in 2026.
quote: It hasn't received significant updates in a long time and hasn't been actively developed, whereas Bases are improving over time.

claim: Obsidian 1.10 beta added Bases APIs and now displays notes as tables, cards, and lists, with Maps as the first official community-built custom view.
quote: display dynamic views of your notes as tables, cards, and now lists

claim: Singular-form properties (tag, alias, cssclass) were deprecated in 1.4 and finally removed in 1.9, requiring power users to migrate to plural list forms.
quote: the following singular-form properties were deprecated in Obsidian 1.4 and removed as default properties in Obsidian 1.9

claim: Bases is the official path forward for database-style views in Obsidian, with an active roadmap covering new view types and formula expansion.
quote: roadmap - Obsidian Help

claim: Obsidian CEO Steph Ango (kepano) avoids folders and organizes via a categories property plus reusable property names (created, author, genre, type, topic) so values stay queryable across categories.
quote: My notes are primarily organized using the `categories` property... I avoid folders because many of my entries belong to more than one area of thought. Property names and values should aim to be reusable across categories.

claim: Long-term Johnny.Decimal users in Obsidian typically combine folder/index hierarchy for primary structure with hierarchical tags reserved for cross-cutting themes that span multiple JD codes.
quote: use tags for similar items that technically belong in different IDs ... organization of objects that don't logically belong in the same part of your J.D system.

claim: Johnny.Decimal caps at exactly 100 permanent addresses (10 areas x 10 categories) and is sold as scaling by permanence rather than infinite expansion, which is the structural ceiling personal-vault adopters hit.
quote: designed to scale. With 100 permanent addresses, you have room to grow.

claim: Practitioner consensus for at-scale vaults: folders only for high-level separation (max two levels), MOCs for navigation, tags as signals/filters rather than primary structure.
quote: Tags ... function as signals, not structure ... useful for discovery and filtering ... but shouldn't be your primary organization system.

claim: A common pattern from large-vault users is to reserve hashtags for note-status indicators (inbox, seedling, evergreen) and use MOCs/links for topical structure.
quote: hashtags solely for indicators of status ... inputs needing to be processed ... seedlings ... evergreen notes.

claim: Nick Milo's LYT framework popularized the rule of thumb to spawn an MOC once 5+ notes cluster on a topic, framing MOCs as the antidote to flat-vault overload.
quote: Links keep notes from getting trapped in a single folder; MOCs keep you from getting lost in the web of links.

claim: The dominant PARA failure mode in Obsidian is Projects and Resources collapsing into each other — Resources becomes a graveyard of interesting-but-not-actionable material while Projects stays artificially clean.
quote: Projects and Resources collapse into each other, with everything interesting becoming a Resource while the Projects folder stays artificially clean.

claim: Hierarchical tag prefixes like #project/active, #project/soon are the de-facto extraction-friendly convention because Dataview can FROM a parent tag and pull all children.
quote: pull all projects using the #project tag, or scope it to a particular status such as #project/soon or #project/active.

claim: Tag-hygiene tooling has emerged specifically because casing/separator inconsistency (snake vs kebab, mixed case) is a known scale problem; bulk tag managers now enforce taxonomy rules across the vault.
quote: bulk renaming, enforcing casing rules (lowercase/uppercase), swapping separators (snake/kebab), and generating master tag lists.

claim: Empirically, vaults with hundreds of tags (one user reported ~400) report no slowdown, so taxonomy bloat is a cognitive/maintenance cost rather than a performance ceiling.
quote: one user with about 400 tags in their vault didn't see any slowdown.

claim: Steph Ango's vault uses 50+ category-specific note templates (Game Studio, Restaurant, Job Interview) anchored by Properties and Bases rather than folder taxonomy.
quote: over 50 templates for notes, for every category of thing, from 'Game Studio' to 'Restaurant' to 'Job Interview'.

claim: python-frontmatter (412 stars, v1.1.0 Jan 2024) parses YAML, JSON, TOML or other front matter via pluggable handlers and recommends utf-8-sig encoding for files with BOM.
quote: Python Frontmatter ... a small package to load and parse files (or just text) with YAML (or JSON, TOML or other) front matter. ... An easy way to do this is by using the `utf-8-sig` encoding.

claim: gray-matter (4.4k stars) is the de-facto JS frontmatter parser used by Astro, Gatsby, VitePress, TinaCMS, Netlify and more, supporting YAML/JSON/TOML/Coffee and custom engines, and claims to be faster than regex-based parsers.
quote: Smarter YAML front matter parser, used by metalsmith, Gatsby, Netlify, Assemble, mapbox-gl, phenomic, vuejs vitepress, TinaCMS, Shopify Polaris, Ant Design, Astro, hashicorp, garden, slidev, saber, sourcegraph, and many others. ... Parses YAML by default but can also parse JSON Front Matter, Coffee Front Matter, TOML Front Matter

claim: Recent gray-matter open issues flag a js-yaml prototype-pollution vulnerability via bundled dependency (#178, Nov 2025), ESM/Vite SSR import-as-function failures (#181, Feb 2026), and improperly cached invalid frontmatter (#166, #174).
quote: Security: js-yaml prototype-pollution vulnerability via bundled dependency (#178) - Opened Nov 16, 2025 ... ESM/Vite SSR compatibility issue - 'is not a function' error with import * as matter (#181) - Opened Feb 8, 2026

claim: remark-frontmatter (320 stars, v5.0.0) only recognises and preserves the frontmatter block — it does NOT parse the YAML/TOML payload, so pipelines must compose it with vfile-matter or remark-mdx-frontmatter to get usable metadata.
quote: this plugin handles frontmatter syntax but crucially does not parse that frontmatter as say YAML or TOML ... combine this plugin with utilities like `vfile-matter` for YAML parsing, or use `remark-mdx-frontmatter`

claim: obsidiantools (553 stars, v0.11.0 July 2025) exposes vault.get_front_matter, vault.get_tags and a NetworkX graph for analytics, supporting nested tags but assuming the 'shortest path when possible' link mode.
quote: Tags via `vault.get_tags(<NOTE>)` or `vault.tags_index`. Nested tags are supported. ... The package is built to support the 'shortest path when possible' option for links.

claim: obsidiantools' tag extractor strips code blocks, escaped \#, and wikilink headers before regex matching, returns only the top tag of nested chains by default, and falls back to an empty dict on invalid YAML — wrapping python-frontmatter underneath.
quote: Tags inside code blocks are ignored ... Remove[s] wikilinks to prevent false positives from header links ... only returns the highest level of nested tags. If `True`, returns all levels. ... If parsing fails due to invalid YAML, it returns an empty dict.

claim: obsidian-export (1.3k stars, v25.3.0 March 2025) is a Rust CLI/library that converts a vault to CommonMark, with --frontmatter=always|never controls and --skip-tags / --only-tags filters, but errors on recursive [[A]]<->[[B]] embeds unless --no-recursive-embeds is set.
quote: frontmatter is preserved as-is. Alternatively, '--frontmatter=always' inserts empty frontmatter when needed, while '--frontmatter=never' removes it entirely. ... It's possible to end up with recursive embeds when two notes embed each other.

claim: py-obsidianmd / pyomd (312 stars, no tagged releases) is the main Python library for batch-moving metadata between YAML frontmatter and Dataview-style inline (key:: value) fields, with an explicit warning to back up the vault before use.
quote: move metadata between frontmatter and inline ... ⚠️ Consider backing up your vault before using the library, to avoid any risk of data loss.

claim: Dataview defines two metadata channels — YAML frontmatter and inline fields written either as `key:: value` or `[key:: value]` — meaning extractors that only parse frontmatter will miss a large class of Obsidian metadata.
quote: Inline Fields are a Dataview feature which allow you to write metadata directly inline in your markdown document via Key:: Value syntax.

claim: The Rust ecosystem offers gray_matter (a port of jonschlinkert's), fronma, yaml-front-matter, secretfader/matter and frontmatter-gen as front-matter crates supporting YAML/TOML/JSON via opt-in cargo features.
quote: gray_matter ... A tool for easily extracting front matter out of a string. It is a fast Rust implementation of gray-matter. Parses YAML, JSON, TOML and support for custom parsers.

claim: fronma is a typed Rust front-matter parser whose YAML support is the default feature while TOML and JSON require enabling cargo feature flags, useful when build size matters in a Rust pipeline.
quote: This library supports YAML (supported by default feature), TOML (requires toml feature), and JSON (requires json feature).

claim: Astro content collections use Zod via defineCollection() with z.object schemas and z.coerce.date() to validate and type-coerce frontmatter at build time, including reference() for cross-collection links.
quote: schema: z.object({ title: z.string(), description: z.string(), pubDate: z.coerce.date(), updatedDate: z.coerce.date().optional(), })

claim: Eleventy v3 introduced eleventyDataSchema, a library-agnostic hook that lets users plug Zod (or any validator) into the data cascade so frontmatter can be checked alongside global/directory data.
quote: eleventyDataSchema: function(data) { let result = z.object({ draft: z.boolean().or(z.undefined()), }).safeParse(data); if(result.error) { throw fromZodError(result.error); } }

claim: remark-lint-frontmatter-schema (73 stars) validates YAML frontmatter against a JSON Schema in a remark pipeline, with in-file $schema associations and IDE underlining.
quote: Validate your Markdown frontmatter data against a JSON schema — remark-lint rule plugin

claim: Yamale (764 stars) is a Python YAML schema validator with built-in str(), int(min,max), num(), bool(), day() for YYYY-MM-DD, timestamp(), list(), enum(), regex() and include() for reusable schemas.
quote:
name: str()
age: int(max=200)
height: num()
awesome: bool()

claim: mdschema (62 stars) declares frontmatter rules in YAML with name/type/format/optional fields, supporting string, number, boolean, array, date and formats like email/url.

Dataview infers eight field types from frontmatter — Text, Number, Boolean, Date (ISO8601 YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm), Duration, Link (must be quoted as "[[Page]]" in YAML), List, and Object — and the type drives which functions and accessors (.year, .month) are available.
quote: Format: YYYY-MM[-DDTHH:mm:ss.nnn+ZZ] ... Accessible properties: .year, .month, .day, .hour, .minute, .second

claim: Obsidian Bases recognises Strings, Numbers, Booleans, Dates (date("2025-01-01"), today(), now()), Lists, Objects, Links (auto-detected wikilinks or link("name","display")) and a File object exposing file.ctime/mtime/tags/ext/size for filters and formulas.
quote: Dates: Created via date("2025-01-01 12:00:00") or today() ... Links: Wikilinks in frontmatter automatically recognized; created via link("filename")

claim: pydantic_yaml parses YAML directly into Pydantic BaseModels with str-Enum subclasses for status fields, nested models, and parse_yaml_raw_as()/to_yaml_str() round-tripping.
quote: class MyEnum(str, Enum): a = "a" b = "b" ... class MyModel(BaseModel): x: int = 1 e: MyEnum = MyEnum.a m: InnerModel = InnerModel()

claim: astro-editor (445 stars) reads a project's Zod content-collection schemas and turns them into typed frontmatter forms, demonstrating that the schema doubles as both validator and UI source-of-truth.
quote: Astro Editor reads your Zod schemas and transforms frontmatter into smart forms. Get validation, proper field types, and MDX component insertion

claim: markdownlint-cli2 has a frontMatter regex option to skip the YAML block during markdown linting, so it pairs with — rather than replaces — schema validators that handle the YAML payload itself.
quote: frontMatter parameter that defines a RegExp used to match and ignore any front matter at the beginning of a document

claim: AI Tagger Universe (84 stars) writes AI-generated tags into note frontmatter, supports 15+ cloud and local LLM providers, offers a hybrid mode that matches existing vault tags before suggesting new ones, and configurable hierarchical depth (1-3 levels) with naming conventions like kebab-case and snake_case.
quote: Automatically generate intelligent tags for your Obsidian notes using AI. This plugin analyzes your content and adds relevant tags to your note's frontmatter

claim: A 2025 case-study workflow uses Gemma 3 12B via LM Studio plus AI Tagger Universe constrained against a predefined Tag List note, then chains Auto Note Mover to file notes by tag — a retrieval-augmented-taxonomy pattern.
quote: I found that predefining tags ensures consistency when filing notes into folders. So, I created a new note named 'Tag List' in the root folder of my vault and filled it with all the tags I wanted the AI to use.

claim: Smart Connections (4.9k stars) is the dominant embeddings-based PKM plugin and offers a Connect Pro tier for bulk metadata updates, but its primary axis is semantic similarity rather than tag generation.
quote: Chat with your notes & see links to related content with AI embeddings. Use local models or 100+ via APIs like Claude, Gemini, ChatGPT & Llama 3

claim: Obsidian Copilot (6.8k stars) is the most-starred chat-with-vault plugin, focused on Vault QA over chat with optional embeddings rather than auto-generating tags or frontmatter.
quote: Search your vault with chat, no setup required. Embeddings are optional.

claim: An active 2025 Copilot enhancement request explicitly asks the Vault QA indexer to read YAML frontmatter tags and aliases so AI answers align with existing taxonomy — frontmatter is read-context for chat plugins, not yet a generation target.
quote: Obsidian's built-in search already respects tags and aliases, and bringing Copilot's QA into alignment removes user confusion and leverages existing vault organization

claim: AI Knowledge Filler (Feb 2026) ships a system prompt that turns Claude Projects, GPT custom instructions, or a CLI into a deterministic generator of vault-ready files with validated YAML, fixed heading hierarchy, WikiLinks and domain classification across 8 file types and 30+ domains.
quote: Instead of asking AI to answer questions, you ask it to generate Obsidian-ready files — with validated YAML metadata, correct heading hierarchy, WikiLinks, and domain classification.

claim: A widely-shared 2025 blog post documents a Python+Claude API script that reads existing frontmatter, asks Claude to extract/infer title, categories, tags, status and priority, parses the YAML response, and merges back — preserving existing fields over generated ones; author argues the LLM beats keyword tagging because it infers semantic context (a BTRFS-on-Arch note auto-tags linux/filesystem/arch).
quote: asks Claude to analyze the content and generate appropriate frontmatter. It adds stuff like title, category, tags, status, priority

claim: MCPVault (renamed to @bitbonsai/mcpvault at Obsidian's request in March 2026) provides AST-aware YAML parsing so unmodified frontmatter fields keep original formatting, plus a list_all_tags tool that scans frontmatter and inline #hashtags with occurrence counts — a foundation for retrieval-augmented tagging via Claude.
quote: New `list_all_tags` tool scans the vault for all frontmatter tags and inline `#hashtags` with occurrence counts

claim: MarkusPfundstein's mcp-obsidian server (3.5k stars) exposes patch_content that lets Claude insert text relative to a frontmatter field, enabling targeted metadata edits via the Obsidian Local REST API plugin.
quote: patch_content: Insert content into an existing note relative to a heading, block reference, or frontmatter field.

claim: Frontmatter Generator (46 stars) is a non-LLM but structured baseline: users define JSON or JS-expression templates that auto-populate YAML on save, often paired with Dataview, increasingly used as a deterministic validation layer beneath LLM-generated output.
quote: It could be a json or a javascript expression that return an object.

claim: Metadata Auto Classifier targets the same niche bound to OpenAI GPT models and surfaces both tags and arbitrary frontmatter fields through Obsidian commands, letting users fetch suggestions on demand rather than on save.
quote: automates metadata management in Obsidian by analyzing note content and generating relevant tags and frontmatter fields

claim: Auto Tag plugin (66 stars) is the simpler OpenAI-only option that analyses full note or selection and writes tags into frontmatter, creating frontmatter when missing.
quote: Automatic Tag Generation: Analyze the entire note or just a selected portion to generate relevant tags.

claim: Standalone Python scripts (e.g. undergroundpost/obsidian-auto-tagger, 1 star) implement the batch pattern: walk recently-modified notes, send to Ollama or OpenAI, and reconcile against existing vault tags for consistency — a common DIY alternative to plugins.
quote: Analyzes existing tags in your vault for consistency

claim: Tag explosion is a real anti-pattern: tagging every noun (#glue, #wood, #saw) creates noise, not signal; recommended remedies are lowercase + singular tags, autocomplete, and tagging sparingly.
quote: Tag sparingly — It's easy to overdo it with tags. Just like with backlinks, less is often more. Keep your tags all lowercase. Use singular tags. Utilize Autocomplete when tagging.

claim: A 2024 forum poster reports a recurring cycle of PKM bloat-then-restart: too many notes, too many ways to link them, leading to unusable vaults despite repeated fresh starts with simpler intentions.
quote: Every attempt, over decades, from pen and paper to Obsidian to Notion, has ended in a mess that becomes unusable. Once the system gets bloated, I start over with the best intentions, trying to keep things simple.

claim: Hacker News commenters describe Obsidian PKM over-engineering as cyclical influencer-driven complexity; remedy is to drop elaborate frameworks and use a minimal daily journal plus search.
quote: the more I learned, the more I realized a majority of it was cyclical... indulgence in it happens at the expense of getting any real work done.

claim: Tags suffer a granularity-ambiguity problem (dog vs dogs vs pets vs mammals) that breaks retrieval unless autocomplete or a controlled vocabulary is enforced.
quote: I think tags are confusing, for reasons that have been adequately discussed elsewhere (Do I tag this document 'dog'? 'dogs'? 'pets?' 'mammals?')

claim: Real users report frontmatter property sprawl from imports/plugins (200+ stale fields) and lack of native bulk-rename/remove tools, forcing error-prone manual cleanup.
quote: I had 200+ property fields...most of them are useless being able to remove properties would be great... I used a plugin to add date fields...the plugin didn't offer a way to remove the Properties it had created.

claim: Tag Wrangler's own docs warn that bulk tag renames are irreversible, can silently merge tags (losing provenance), and may partial-fail under sync; backup before renaming is the recommended remedy.
quote: Renaming a tag is potentially an irreversible operation: you may wish to back up your data before beginning a rename... If background sync causes files to change during a rename, Tag Wrangler may skip changed file(s), resulting in a partial rename.

claim: A 2024 user reflection recommends a controlled-vocabulary tag system (Library of Congress style) and reserves frontmatter properties strictly for objective minutiae (URLs, dates, author, series) to avoid sprawl.
quote: everything you're about to read is just me thinking out loud about how I've been using Obsidian lately after a lot of experimentation... Reserve [properties] for minutiae like URLs, publication date...author name, series, etc.

claim: Community guidance on the inline-vs-property-tag split: note-level classification belongs in frontmatter; sentence/section-level markup belongs inline as #tags, and tags should be migrated to stable fields once patterns stabilize.
quote: If you want to tag a note, it belongs in the frontmatter, if you want to tag a sentence or a section, it belongs in the text... transition tags into more stable fields once patterns emerge.

claim: The 2023 Obsidian Properties feature rollout broke existing tag-bearing frontmatter for many users — a concrete instance of metadata-rot from a platform schema change.
quote: Properties update broke my meta tags

claim: Users report phantom tag bloat where dozens of tags appear unprompted, illustrating that tag indexes can drift from actual content and require periodic auditing.
quote: Suddenly many dozens of tags I did not create

claim: A long-running multi-page feature request shows persistent demand for safe, native bulk tag rename/delete across files — confirming this is a chronic pain point, not a one-off.
quote: Tag Mass Action: Add, Rename, and Delete a tag in multiple files

claim: Forum threads document users hitting the wall of needing to rename a frontmatter property value vault-wide and resorting to ripgrep/sd or external scripts because no native command existed.
quote: How can I rename a frontmatter / property value in all my notes?
